speaker: interviewer
text: --he asked you. No, no. That's not gonna work. No, that's not gonna work. Where's the slate? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you got dark blue, Ernie?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, something dark-- anything-- the darkest shirt you got.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or is it too sporty? No, he's only got dress shirt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, then maybe I'll do without the tie then-- just a-- something like this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you seem to want something more formal?

speaker: interviewer
text: Talk. Today is May the 19th, 1995. The survivor we're interviewing is Ernest Weiss. The interviewer is Leonia Kurgan; city/state is LA, California, in the USA. And this interview is gonna be conducted in English. Thank you so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What must I say again-- adjustment? My name is Dr. Leonia Kurgan, and I am interviewing the survivor, Ernie Weiss, in Los Angeles, on the 19th of May, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell that?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your given name, Ernie, when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was the same. It was Ernest. It was always Ernest. Except under the Hungarians, it was Erno, which was spelled E-R-N-O, with two dots on top of the O.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: --under the Czechs. It was in Mukacevo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me a-- a sense of what your life was like before the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Did you live in a house, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was-- it was an-- an apartment, yeah. And I lived with my family. We were-- my father and my mother. And I had two brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The oldest one was born in 1922, and the other one was born in 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he worked wherever he could. He didn't have a trade. Wherever he could get a job, that's what he did. I guess you can say a handyman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And what was the-- the life-- the everyday life? What did you do? Where did you play? Who did you play with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I-- just like any other kids-- you know, played-- played on the street. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Any particular game that you liked playing?

speaker: interviewee
text: None that-- I can't remember right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you most attached to in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was attached to my older brother more than the middle one.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Isidor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you like about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We were able to communicate better.

speaker: interviewer
text: You could talk to him easier?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he didn't come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] Do you remember some things you spoke about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He-- he always wanted to come to America. And he said that-- he said that me and him, we gonna go to America. And he was an-- he was an auto mechanic. He said he was gonna open up a shop, and-- and then bring over the rest of the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he-- he even spoke English. He was self-taught. He-- he spoke English, and he was well read. He always liked to read. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- and, you know, he-- he used to love movies-- and-- American movies. And he-- he just loved America. And he said that-- that we gonna go to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what were the conditions like where you lived in-- in-- in Munkatsh?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, it was a hard life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. My father was a-- was more religious. My father was religious. But the-- the kids were not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to Hebrew school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we went to Hebrew school. And-- and at home, of course, we kept a kosher home. Yes. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a favorite food that you liked?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. At-- at that time, I didn't think of anything favorite. You know, as long as there was something to eat-- and I was always a good eater. I always loved food. And I still do. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the family do for-- for-- you know, for fun? Did you go on any outings, or-- or mix with the neighbors, or-- or have parties, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we mixed. We mixed with the neighbors-- no parties, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- did people get together and talk, or-- or--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when you say the war broke out-- or the-- the time, you know, just before we were taken away?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when war broke out in '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in '39. In '39, I-- I was 10-- 12 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: 12 years old, yes, yes. Did you go with your brother to the movies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We went to the movies. We used to like the movies. Yeah, we used to go-- something that was not supposed to do. It's Saturday afternoon. [LAUGHS] But we went to the movies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. You didn't tell your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: But actually, the movie house was full--

speaker: interviewee
text: --with Jewish people on a Saturday afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard to be a Jewish boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't remember so much under the Czechs. But the Hungarians, when they occupied my city, in 1938, I seem to remember the anti-Semitism was bigger under the Hungarians.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, we used to get beat up.

speaker: interviewee
text: By whom? You know, they used to call-- call you over, and, you know, like, you know, they want to ask you a question. And then they beat you up, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, so, I just ran away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I just-- just took it. Yeah. You see, when I was younger, I used to wear little payots-- not long ones, just a little bit. So, you know, they knew I was Jewish. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then did you cut them off ever, or-- or-- did you stay with the payots?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, for a little I, you know, for a little bit longer, and then I cut 'em off, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. How were you dressed as a little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a little boy I was dressed-- you see, I tell you. I-- I quit school. I quit school and went to work, so I can buy nice clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gee, I don't remember-- probably about 13, 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: That it was important to you to-- to-- to look good?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, and then what did you do when you quit school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- [LAUGHS] I did all kinds of things. I worked for-- for a company that packs coal into-- weigh 'em up, by like, you know, like kilo, you know, into a bag. And-- and then get 'em ready for-- for shipment to the stores. Yeah, it was, like, in a wholesale.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it goes to the stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Did you have any more family? Was it just your family? Or were there brothers? Or were there uncles, and aunts, or grandparents? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. I-- I only knew one of my grandparents, from my father. My mother's parents, they died all very young. And they didn't even live in our city. My father moved from another city into our city.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't see them much?

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- no. No, but we did have some cousins. Yeah, we did have some relatives.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was different from Shabbat to the other? You know, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, you know, we-- I-- I went to the temple.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- yes, in the morning. And then we came home. We had a Shabbat meal. That we used to take it like Friday. Friday, late afternoon we used to take it to the bakery. And that's where they made the cholent and the kugel. And then after the temple on Saturday, we brought it home. And we had a Shabbat meal. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Did you have your own room? Or how did you-- did the brothers share? Or how-- how was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: The three-- the three boys were in one room--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Anything else about your life there with your family? Was it a pretty town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was-- it was a pretty town. And actually, it was-- it was an up-to-date town, you know, for those days. Where, you know, it's-- we had everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was available. All you needed is money. We had anything-- anything you can think of was available. As a matter of fact, I remember-- I was just a kid. When I remember-- they used to call it Little America, and Little Palestine-- Little America, because there was everything available; Little Palestine because there was a lot of Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you say everything, what you mean by everything? What kind of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, oranges, bananas, peanuts in the shell, and-- and just-- just anything you can think of.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was quite a prosperous little place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- apples, pears, you know, from fruit--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had cars. Very-- not that-- not that many, but we had cars. We had taxis. We had buses that went from cities-- from different-- to different cities. And then, of course, we had these coach transportation, you know, with a-- like a horse and buggy, but it's a-- it was a-- a coach.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually, it wasn't really closed. There was the top was open. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you use that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I didn't, but I mean, people-- yeah-- it was there. It was available.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. How much did that cost?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows? I-- I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Yeah. What was the money that you used?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, under the Czech, it was korunas, and under the Hungarians it was pengos-- pengo. Yeah, that's the dollar. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Anything else you can think of, or from those days-- prewar?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Well, I also used to love to go for entertainment. Yeah, I used-- that's right. You asked me what I liked to do. I used to like to go to the circus, and-- and like, an amusement park, you know, the merry-go-round and the swing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they come just sometimes, or were they there all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At-- not all the time. That-- I mean, you know, they-- for the summer-- for the summer and the spring, you know. And I used to be a daredevil. I used to go on that swing, you know. I used to drive it up. That used to put a brake on me, [LAUGHS] because, you know, when you go too high, then they brake you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you shouldn't be so high.

speaker: interviewer
text: You used to put your car up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went with my brother, and went with my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they mostly Jewish boys that you were friendly with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. Of course, you know, I was friendly. You know, we had neighbors that weren't Jewish, of course, I was friendly with them, too. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Were your parents happy with each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah, I-- I would think so. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] So what was the next important thing that happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't know exactly-- you mean, still while-- while I was still home?

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Do you remember when war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, I-- I remember where, you know, when the Germans came in, into our city. And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: What they look like? Well, I mean, you know, it-- they-- that-- first, you know, we didn't know it. They were just, you know, soldiers, you know? But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the first time you saw the soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went into the city, you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: --into downtown, like-- and so, all of a sudden, we see, you know, German soldiers. And at that time, I didn't know the difference between a soldier and an SS, you know? But-- or soon after, the order came out that the Jews have to wear a star. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know there was a war on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, even before we were start wearing stars, we were neighboring with Poland-- Galicia. And they-- they were taken away already. And a lot of them escaped and came into our city. And we-- we were hiding them, and we fed 'em, and took care of them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you wear a star? What did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like a Star of David--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what-- what-- where did you get them from?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- good question. I don't remember. But I know we-- we somehow, we got 'em. How we got 'em, where we got 'em, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And everybody had to wear it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it didn't feel good. It was, you know, like you're being singled out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if you, you know, if-- they can stop you any time if you don't wear 'em and they see your identification; and if you are Jewish, and you don't wear 'em you're in trouble.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't know. But, I mean, as far as I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And what did your parents say when all this was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- it was sad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Had you had a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewer
text: --was the age when you were-- you must have been about 13.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I-- I-- we just-- I just never did. I--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I missed out on that. Yeah. And I-- I also stopped going to cheder, you know, which is like Hebrew school over here. And I never prepared for it. And I never been through with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then, anyway, then they took us-- they-- from everybody moved from their home into the ghetto, and we were there for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the ghetto in relation to where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- it's called the-- the Yiddish [NON-ENGLISH], which is the Jewish street. And so, we were all cramped in there. And you--

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I know, you know. I don't know how-- I don't remember how far back it went or-- I just know we were just in-- in there and-- and then not too long after that, we all went to-- they all-- we marched through the city to a brick factory. And we were there for a few days, I guess.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody-- all the Jews-- the whole--

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I guess the reason for that is because the-- the train came right through there, into the brick factory. And then they-- from there, we were sent to Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the-- from that-- from the ghetto to the brick factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And before you go to Birkenau, what was it like in the ghetto? Did you-- did you live in one room, or-- you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, all in one room-- all in one room, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All in one room, yes. And, to go back a little bit, you-- you mentioned that you left school because you wanted to dress nicely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were now-- so this was round about when you were 12, 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that? That must have been about-- you were born in 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's about 1940. So had you started going out with girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I-- I didn't have enough courage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I didn't feel-- I didn't feel that I was-- that I had enough money to take out girls. So I actually never had a date before-- before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. So you were all in one room, your family, your-- and-- and were there other people there as well in the ghetto-- in your-- in that room where you stayed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I don't-- I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Did you take anything with you to that-- to the ghetto from your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we took just whatever we could.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just-- just clothing-- just clothing. I don't think we took any-- anything. The furniture, I don't remember about taking any of the furniture. I really don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had no-- did you have a favorite possession or something that you took with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] No, I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But then we, you know, we still took-- whatever we took to the ghetto, we still took it along to that brick factory, because, you know, we thought that we didn't know where we are going. But after that, everything was left. They took everything away-- just what you had on you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know. I don't remember. Maybe I wore a suit, or maybe just a-- a sport coat and a-- I don't remember. It's too far back.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Then we were-- we were boxed in-- the whole family, you know. And they-- boxed cars-- and we still didn't know where we are going. We didn't know until we arrived there, which was Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: [SIGHS] How many people in the boxed car-- a lot of--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember, but it was-- it was packed. It was--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was probably something up at the top. There may have been a window, or like, a screen. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So then when we-- when we arrived-- when we arrived there, and we got out, so they separated men, women-- Who was there?

speaker: interviewer
text: --look like? Well-- what he look like? What did it look like? What did you see as you got out of the box--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well, you saw, you know, you saw barracks. You know, you saw-- you saw the barracks already.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And you know, my mother went with the women. And luckily, my father, and my other brother, and I, we wind up together for a short time. Well, we-- we were together there in Birkenau. But a short time after that, they made up a transport to go to Warsaw. And we were still together, the three of us. So we were together in Warsaw, in the camp-- in the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the other brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the other brother? He was taken away--

speaker: interviewee
text: Isidor was taken away, like, in the military. But the Jews were not in the military. The Jews were in forced labor camps. So he-- he was working in forced labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was he separated-- from the rest of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no, he was taken away before. Like, you know, when you become of the age to go to the army. So he went into the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: He may have even had a-- an army uniform, but he didn't-- he worked as forced labor for the military.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that, Ernie?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe that was either '42 or '43-- in 1942 or 1943.
